From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

English comedian (1901–1957)

"Arthur McEvoy" redirects here. For the Canadian politician, see [Arthur J. McEvoy](/wiki/Arthur_J._McEvoy "Arthur J. McEvoy"). For the Olympic cricketer, see [Arthur MacEvoy](/wiki/Arthur_MacEvoy "Arthur MacEvoy").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article includes a list of [general references](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#General_references "Wikipedia:Citing sources"), but **it lacks sufficient corresponding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")**. Please help to [improve](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Reliability "Wikipedia:WikiProject Reliability") this article by [introducing](/wiki/Wikipedia:When_to_cite "Wikipedia:When to cite") more precise citations. *(May 2015)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

| Frank Randle | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Born | Arthur Hughes  (1901-01-30)30 January 1901  [Aspull](/wiki/Aspull "Aspull"), England |
| Died | 7 July 1957(1957-07-07) (aged 56)  [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool "Blackpool"), England |
| Occupation | Comedian/Comic actor |
| Spouse | May Annie Victoria Douglas |

**Frank Randle** (born Arthur Hughes, also known as **Arthur McEvoy** or **Arthur Twist**; 30 January 1901 – 7 July 1957) was an English comedian.[[1]](#cite_note-1) A contemporary of fellow [Lancastrians](/wiki/Lancashire "Lancashire") [George Formby](/wiki/George_Formby,_Jr. "George Formby, Jr.") and [Gracie Fields](/wiki/Gracie_Fields "Gracie Fields"), he was regarded as more subversive, perhaps explaining why the immense popularity he enjoyed during his lifetime has not survived him.[[2]](#cite_note-independent-2)

## Life and career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Randle&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life and career")]

Randle was born in [Aspull](/wiki/Aspull "Aspull"), near [Wigan](/wiki/Wigan "Wigan"), Lancashire, to unmarried domestic servant Rhoda Heathcote Hughes (1879-1965); his father was unrecorded.[[3]](#cite_note-3) He left school aged 13 and worked in menial jobs until two years later when he joined an [acrobatic](/wiki/Acrobatics "Acrobatics") troupe.[[4]](#cite_note-4) He took the name Arthur McEvoy after his mother married mechanic Richard McEvoy in 1913.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 1928, Randle began to tour as a comedian, principally in Lancashire and Northern England.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Randle appeared on stage carrying a red warning lamp, similar to the type found around road works, declaring "Look what some dam'd fool left in’t road".[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMather200319-7) He developed his own show, *Randle's Scandals*, which in the 1950s featured [Roy Castle](/wiki/Roy_Castle "Roy Castle").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Randle's mischievous wit led to a running conflict with Harry Barnes, a police chief constable of the Lancashire seaside resort of [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool "Blackpool"), who frequently banned him from performing in the town's venues. He was prosecuted in 1952 on four charges of obscenity and fined £10 on each count.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Randle responded to his critics in robust fashion, frequently throwing his false teeth into the audience and once bombarding Blackpool from an aeroplane with toilet rolls (according to an episode of *Rude Britannia*, broadcast by the BBC on 15 June 2010, the toilet roll bombardment actually took place over [Accrington](/wiki/Accrington "Accrington"), not Blackpool).[[10]](#cite_note-10) Randle's police charge sheet is lodged with [Lancashire Archives](/wiki/Lancashire_Archives "Lancashire Archives").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

On the outbreak of the Second World War, having failed his medical to join the [RAF](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force"), Randle joined the [Home Guard](/wiki/Home_Guard_(United_Kingdom) "Home Guard (United Kingdom)") and established a career in film. His iconoclastic portrayal of the underdog, flouting authority and disrupting the establishment, found a ready audience in a population suffering the privations of war. He took equity in [John E. Blakeley](/wiki/John_E._Blakeley "John E. Blakeley")'s [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester")-based [Mancunian Film Studios](/wiki/Mancunian_Films "Mancunian Films"), appearing in eight of its productions. In his last film, *[It's a Grand Life](/wiki/It%27s_a_Grand_Life "It's a Grand Life")* (1953), his co-star was [Diana Dors](/wiki/Diana_Dors "Diana Dors").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/75/Frankrandle%27sgrave.jpg/250px-Frankrandle%27sgrave.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankrandle%27sgrave.jpg)

Frank Randle's grave

Although he was reportedly taking £1,000 a week [at the box office] at [Hulme Hippodrome](/wiki/Hulme_Hippodrome "Hulme Hippodrome") in 1950,[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13) with the decline of [Variety](/wiki/Music_hall "Music hall") in the 1950s Randle's career declined. Pressed by debts and tax arrears, and suffering from the consequences of a life of [alcohol abuse](/wiki/Alcohol_abuse "Alcohol abuse"), he reportedly owed the Inland Revenue £56,000 in 1954[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13) was made [bankrupt](/wiki/Bankrupt "Bankrupt") by the tax authorities in 1955.[[2]](#cite_note-independent-2)

He married May Annie Victoria Douglas, known as *Queenie*, in 1928 in [Greenwich](/wiki/Greenwich "Greenwich"), London. There were no children but [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester") artist [Arthur Delaney](/wiki/Arthur_Delaney "Arthur Delaney") was alleged to be Randle's illegitimate son by fellow performer Genevieve Delaney (also known as Eve Delaney). This has been verified by DNA as incorrect.

Randle's comedy achievement was celebrated in "Grin up North", a major touring exhibition that looked at the unique Northern sense of humour. He was most recently featured in an episode of BBC 4's *Rude Britannia* shown in June 2010.[[2]](#cite_note-independent-2)

In 2007, a celebratory plaque paid for by members of the Cuthbert Club was unveiled to Randle on Blackpool's [North Pier](/wiki/North_Pier,_Blackpool "North Pier, Blackpool").[[14]](#cite_note-14) In 2010, the same organisation paid for the refurbishment of Randle's gravestone, which was unveiled in July 2010.

## Filmography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Randle&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Filmography")]

* *[Somewhere in England](/wiki/Somewhere_in_England_(film) "Somewhere in England (film)")* (1940)
* *[Somewhere in Camp](/wiki/Somewhere_in_Camp "Somewhere in Camp")* (1942)
* *[Somewhere on Leave](/wiki/Somewhere_on_Leave "Somewhere on Leave")* (1943)
* *[Somewhere in Civvies](/wiki/Somewhere_in_Civvies "Somewhere in Civvies")* (1943)
* *[Home Sweet Home](/wiki/Home_Sweet_Home_(1945_film) "Home Sweet Home (1945 film)")* (1945)
* *[Holidays with Pay](/wiki/Holidays_with_Pay "Holidays with Pay")* (1948)
* *[When You Come Home](/wiki/When_You_Come_Home "When You Come Home")* (1948)
* *[Somewhere in Politics](/wiki/Somewhere_in_Politics "Somewhere in Politics")* (1948)
* *[School for Randle](/wiki/School_for_Randle "School for Randle")* (1949)
* *[It's a Grand Life](/wiki/It%27s_a_Grand_Life "It's a Grand Life")* (1953)
